title: Business council out of step with youth on AI
full_text:
The chief executive of Australia’s business lobby group and a bunch of vested-interest stakeholders declared the nation could risk a $1.4 trillion boom if it doesn’t make artificial intelligence and data centre investment a “national priority”.
Their views are out of step with a growing movement of people who actually want AI regulated for the opposite reasons.
Pope Leo himself spoke up on Monday night – in his first major theological document since he became head of the Catholic Church – declaring AI needed to be “disarmed” to prevent it driving wars, destroying jobs and dehumanising decision-making.
At its best, AI promises to drive human capability and creativity by automating repetitive work and making complex technology tasks easy. But at its worst, AI threatens jobs – particularly for young people – without creating flow-on industries that could create more work.
Fewer workers means fewer taxpayers, so the government should be making it a national priority but not for the reasons cited by the delegation that included Robin Khuda, founder of data centre operator AirTrunk, and two law firms cutting graduate intakes this year: Allens, and Gilbert + Tobin.
Indeed, it’s young people – the digital natives who have never known a world without smartphones – who seem to best see the risks GenAI now poses.
Former Google boss Eric Schmidt was booed by students as he spoke about AI during a speech recently at a University of Arizona graduation ceremony. A few years earlier, his speech would have been met with rousing support.
Rarely, if ever, has there been such a large cohort of university graduates staring down the barrel of a deeply uncertain future.
Australia on Monday had its first reported AI-related death threat, allegedly to WiseTech chief executive Zubin Appoo after the firm started cutting jobs. Founder Richard White was brutal in his opinion that AI agents could do a job as well as humans but it “never goes to sleep, never complains, never asks for a pay rise, doesn’t go on holidays, works 24/7”.
Job cuts have been coming thick and fast across the tech space. Meta, the owner of Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp, last week laid off 8000 people – 10 per cent of its 80,000-strong workforce – as the company pivots towards AI.
At the same time, the company plans to spend $US145bn ($202.3bn) this year to build the vast fleet of data centres required to attain “superintelligence”. Oracle recently combined record profits with record job cuts.
In Australia, WiseTech, Atlassian and the banks have flagged job cuts with AI elements.
Then let’s take a look at those data centres. Clearly, we need them, and we have some built and run by CDC Data Centres and NextDC to name a few.
But how many, and at what environmental cost, is yet to be determined. These centres require vast quantities of constant energy. The largest AI data centre currently under construction is Meta’s Hyperion project, which will scale up to 5GW of power – the same amount required by Greater Sydney on a hot day.
In Ireland, data centres now account for 30 per cent of the nation’s electricity use. Is this what we want for our country? We are a nation with coal-fired plants beyond their use-by date and a policy of no nuclear power. Our efforts to store renewables must be seriously questioned if the debacle that is the $42bn-and-counting Snowy Hydro 2.0 energy storage project is anything to go by.
Energy Minister Chris Bowen admitted to the ABC on Tuesday morning that he remains unclear what an independent review of what Snowy Hydro 2.0 will cost, and when the many-years-delayed project will be finished.
OpenAI boss Sam Altman is in Australia at the moment, talking up the prospect of this country becoming a “data centre capital of the world” because of its natural resources and energy. Clearly, he hasn’t read up on how badly thought through promises on net-zero targets have left our energy strategy in disarray.
Snow job on costs The Australian National Audit Office was supposed to update the government on the cost blowouts at Snowy 2.0 this month but today it pushed back that timeline by a month.
It could be more than that.
Bowen said he didn’t know when it would actually be delivered, telling ABC Radio National: “I envisioned it’ll be this year.” “(It’s) very complicated, one of the world’s most complicated engineering projects, and (Snowy Hydro) have been doing that review. I haven’t received a report. When I receive a report, not long after that, it will be made public,” Bowen said.
Snowy Hydro chief executive Dennis Barnes was due to face Senate estimates some time on Tuesday evening but the government seemed to be stringing out earlier speakers (deliberately perhaps?) and so missed this paper’s deadline.
The ANAO is examining whether the Albanese government’s decision in 2023 to shift Italian construction giant Webuild onto a cost plus project, which means it will make a profit regardless of time and cost overruns, was “ informed by sound planning and advice”.
It is also considering how well SHL is managing the conflict of interest Webuild now has to get Snowy wrapped up promptly, by reviewing if it’s “effectively managing contract performance”.
The real cost of building Snowy Hydro 2.0 could be as much as $42bn including the necessary transmission lines or $20bn for the Snowy project alone, according to energy economists Bruce Mountain and Ted Woodley.
That’s $40bn more than what former prime minister Malcolm Turnbull announced in 2017. It’s already five years behind his forecast completion date and many construction experts believe there is a “good chance” it might be technically impossible to finish.
Webuild won the project to build Snowy 2.0 by pitching something about a billion dollars cheaper than the rival bidding group, coming in with a lower price due to its “never done before” angled tunnels, rather than the standard “L” shape build.
It still hasn’t been done.
The project has been a debacle from the start. One of its boring machines has become stuck, and another has been repeatedly out of action due to a cracked drill head. Shocking footage of flooding in the tunnels has also emerged.
Professionals Australia, the union representing the project’s engineers, supervisors, surveyors and superintendents, has raised “serious safety concerns”, about the works.
Those include “an excavator striking a staff member, a truck jackknifing, and multiple environmental incidents that were either underreported or not disclosed to regulators”, the union says.
Woodley is sceptical that any latest costings on Snowy Hydro 2.0 that are driven by SHL would be worth the paper they are printed on anyway.
The company has a “hopeless” track record, he says, including in 2019 when it signed a $5.1bn fixed price contract for the main works (only), which was a billion dollars more than the government had approved for the entire project just five weeks earlier.
Now of course that figure looks minuscule compared to the $12bn cost for the main works projected last time around, which is expected to be blown out of the water by the latest cost estimation.
Snowy Hydro started working on the review of how much it will cost to complete last October, and Bowen said it would be delivered “sooner rather than later.” “But I’m not going to give a promise I can’t keep,” he told the ABC. Well that’s a relief.
SNOWY HYDRO 2.0 COSTINGS Date Announcement Costing Mar 15, 2017 First announcement $2bn May 23, 2017 Senate estimates $2bn Dec 21, 2017 Feasibility study $3.8bn Dec 12, 2018 Final investment decision $3.8bn Feb 26, 2019 Government approval $3.8bn Apr 5, 2019 Main works contract $5.1bn Apr 8, 2019 Senate estimates $4.9bn Sep 2019 Main works EIS $4.6bn Oct 20, 2020 Senate estimates $5.1bn Nov 7, 2022 Senate estimates $5.9bn Aug 31, 2023 Project reset $12bn Source: Bruce Mountain and Ted Woodley
